skill.name: configuration-validator
skill.description: Validates environment variables and configuration files, checks required settings, and helps document deployment configuration safely.
skill.summary: Review environment variables, config files, and deployment settings for missing or unsafe values.
skill.author: CrazyDubya

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, environment, filesystem, network, and secret patterns. Review found these are mostly instructional examples for a configuration validation skill, but the skill intentionally reads and processes .env files, so users should treat it as medium risk and avoid exposing real secrets in outputs.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Environment File Handling
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE. The skill instructs the agent to find and compare .env files and to generate .env.example from an actual .env file. This is legitimate for configuration validation, but it can expose secrets if raw values are read, copied, logged, or written into shared files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The references to .env files are direct and central to the skill workflow. The risk is data exposure during legitimate validation, not confirmed malicious exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Shell Commands Over Configuration Files
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE WITH CONTEXT. The skill provides bash commands using grep, comm, sed, and npm validation commands. These examples appear intended for local configuration checks, but shell execution over user projects can leak filenames, mishandle unusual values, or overwrite .env.example if used without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit, but they are not obfuscated and do not include user-controlled command construction. The residual risk comes from executing them against sensitive local files.

low_findings.title: Network Findings Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The detected URLs are example configuration values and documentation links. No evidence found that the skill performs network requests or sends environment data to a remote service.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear inside illustrative configuration documentation. There is no fetch, curl, webhook, or upload instruction tied to these URLs.

low_findings.title: Secret Names Are Placeholder Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE FOR HARD-CODED SECRET VALUES. The skill lists common names such as DATABASE_URL, API_KEY, and SECRET_KEY to teach validation. No real credential value or exfiltration target is present in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The names match secret patterns, but the surrounding text is documentation and validation guidance. The example values are placeholders rather than active secrets.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE FOR INSTRUCTION OVERRIDE RISK. No evidence found of text claiming system authority, asking the evaluator to ignore instructions, or attempting to change audit behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed file contains normal skill metadata and usage instructions. It does not contain override language or reviewer-directed manipulation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Derived Example File From Real .env
dangerous_patterns.description: The command to strip values from .env into .env.example can still reveal secret names and may overwrite an existing template. Review generated output before sharing it.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is plainly present and writes a file derived from sensitive configuration. It removes values, so the risk is reduced but not eliminated.

dangerous_patterns.title: Recursive Secret Search Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The recursive grep example searches for hardcoded API keys. It is useful for audits but can surface secret material in terminal output.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is a legitimate local audit step, but its output could contain secrets. There is no evidence of remote transmission.

content.value_statement: Missing environment variables and weak config documentation cause deployment failures. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code review required settings, examples, and validation scripts.
actual_capabilities: Finds common configuration files such as .env, .env.example, config files, and settings files.
Compares expected environment variables with project configuration examples.
Suggests runtime validation patterns for Node.js and Python projects.
Checks common format issues, including ports, URLs, booleans, and missing required values.
Helps create documentation for required and optional configuration variables.
Highlights secret hygiene practices such as gitignore coverage and placeholder examples.
limitations: It may need access to sensitive .env files, so outputs should avoid real secret values.
It provides examples and guidance, not a full static analysis engine.
It may require project-specific knowledge to identify every required variable.
Shell examples should be reviewed before running in production repositories.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Web App for Deployment
use_cases.description: Review environment variables, defaults, and documentation before a release.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Configuration Docs
use_cases.description: Create or improve CONFIG.md and .env.example so onboarding is more reliable.

use_cases.title: Audit Config Hygiene in CI
use_cases.description: Identify missing validation scripts and risky secret handling before CI deployment.

prompt_templates.title: Check My Env Example
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this project for missing environment variables. Compare .env.example, config files, and startup code. Do not print real secret values.

prompt_templates.title: Document Required Configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a concise configuration reference for this project. List required and optional variables, expected formats, defaults, and safe placeholder examples.

prompt_templates.title: Add Runtime Validation
prompt_templates.prompt: Find where configuration is loaded and suggest runtime validation. Include type checks for ports, URLs, booleans, and required secrets.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Deployment Config Risk
prompt_templates.prompt: Perform a deployment configuration audit. Check secret hygiene, environment-specific files, validation scripts, documentation gaps, and CI readiness. Redact sensitive values.

output_examples.input: A Node.js service has .env.example, config.js, and a deployment checklist.
output: Missing required variable: JWT_SECRET is used at startup but absent from .env.example.
Format concern: API_URL should include a protocol such as https://.
Documentation gap: CONFIG.md should explain DATABASE_URL format and PORT default.

output_examples.input: A Python app loads settings from environment variables before deployment.
output: Add startup validation for DATABASE_URL, SECRET_KEY, and ALLOWED_HOSTS.
Convert DEBUG to a boolean explicitly instead of relying on a raw string.
Keep real .env values private and publish only placeholders.

output_examples.input: A team wants to create a safe .env.example from local settings.
output: Create placeholders for each key instead of copying real values.
Review generated examples before committing them.
Confirm .env is ignored by version control.

best_practices: Ask the agent to redact real values before summarizing .env findings.
Review shell commands before running them against production or private repositories.
Keep .env.example and configuration documentation updated with every new required variable.
anti_patterns: Do not paste production secrets into prompts or generated documentation.
Do not commit generated .env.example files without checking for leaked values.
Do not assume every required variable is documented unless startup code was reviewed.

faq.question: Does this skill read real .env files?
faq.answer: It can guide review of .env files. Use redaction and avoid exposing real secret values in chat or documentation.

faq.question: Can it create a complete .env.example?
faq.answer: It can help derive one from project variables, but a human should review placeholders and remove sensitive values.

faq.question: Does it support Node.js and Python?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes examples for process.env, os.getenv, and schema-based validation patterns.

faq.question: Will it validate all framework-specific settings?
faq.answer: Not automatically. It provides a workflow that should be adapted to the project framework and deployment platform.

faq.question: Is it safe for production repositories?
faq.answer: It can be useful, but users should redact secrets and review shell commands before execution.

faq.question: What tools does it work with?
faq.answer: The report lists compatibility with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
